blocks.statement: I can identify the bands of the EM spectrum in order (radio → gamma), explain that all travel at c in vacuum, and compute λ from f using c = fλ for any band.

blocks.whatWeKnow: Radar (radio band) and the meteor's atmospheric-entry GLOW (visible) are the SAME kind of wave family. Different f, same physics.
blocks.connection: Day 16 makes this concrete — the meteor's GLOW color (visible light) tells us its temperature (Wien's law).

blocks.markdown:
## Light is a wave — and it has a LOT of relatives

Everything from radio to gamma rays is the same physical thing: a transverse wave of oscillating electric and magnetic fields, traveling at **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** in vacuum. No medium needed — that's how sunlight crosses 150 million km of empty space.

The only difference between the bands is frequency (and therefore wavelength, since **c = fλ** always holds). Walk the spectrum from longest λ to shortest:

| Band | λ (vacuum) | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Radio | > 1 m | Broadcasting, cell, planetary radar (Day 15) |
| Microwave | 1 m to 1 mm | Microwave oven (2.4 GHz), radar |
| Infrared (IR) | 1 mm to 700 nm | Heat, night vision, TV remote |
| Visible | 700 to 400 nm | ROYGBIV — what our eyes detect |
| Ultraviolet (UV) | 400 to 10 nm | Sunburn, sterilization |
| X-ray | 10 to 0.01 nm | Medical imaging |
| Gamma | < 0.01 nm | Nuclear processes, cosmic events |

Same wave equation as Day 3 — just substitute c for v: **λ = c / f** and **f = c / λ**. Master those two moves and you can place any EM wave on the spectrum.

terms.definition: A transverse wave of oscillating electric + magnetic fields. Travels at c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum. No medium needed — EM waves are NOT mechanical, which is why they cross vacuum from the Sun to Earth. Example: light, radio, X-rays, microwaves — all the same kind of wave, different frequencies.
terms.cognate: Sp. onda electromagnética · Pt. onda eletromagnética · HC onn elektwomayetik

terms.definition: The full range of EM frequencies, from low-f radio (km wavelengths) up to high-f gamma rays (sub-nm wavelengths). Example: radio (km), microwave (cm), infrared (μm), VISIBLE (400–700 nm), UV (10s of nm), X-ray (nm), gamma (sub-nm). The bands shade into each other — the names are conventions, not sharp edges.
terms.cognate: Sp. espectro electromagnético · Pt. espectro eletromagnético

terms.definition: The narrow band of EM the human eye detects, from ~400 nm (violet) to ~700 nm (red). The Sun emits its peak intensity here. Example: ROYGBIV — red has the longest visible λ, violet the shortest. Color = wavelength. "Visible" is just our species' window: bees see UV, some snakes see IR.
terms.cognate: Sp. luz visible · Pt. luz visível · HC limyè vizib

blocks.instruction: Walk left-to-right through the EM bands. Which band has the LONGEST wavelength? The HIGHEST frequency? Where does our radar fall? Where does visible light fall?
prompts: Draw the spectrum as a left-to-right strip: radio → microwave → IR → visible → UV → X-ray → gamma.
Label which end has long λ / low f and which has short λ / high f.
Mark where NASA's 2.4 GHz radar sits and where the meteor's visible glow sits.

blocks.prompt: MICROWAVE WAVELENGTH. Your microwave oven operates at f = 2.4 GHz = 2.4 × 10⁹ Hz. What's its wavelength? Which EM band is this?
blocks.answer: λ ≈ 12.5 cm. This is in the MICROWAVE band — sized to make food molecules absorb.

blocks.prompt: VISIBLE GREEN LIGHT. Green light has λ ≈ 530 nm. What's its frequency?
blocks.answer: f ≈ 5.66 × 10¹⁴ Hz ≈ 566 THz — about a half-quadrillion cycles per second.

blocks.prompt: YOUR TURN — X-RAY FREQUENCY. An X-ray has λ ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ m. What's its frequency?
blocks.equationHint: Same move as the green-light example: f = c / λ. Expect a HUGE number — X-rays are near the top of the spectrum.

blocks.title: Why X-rays are dangerous but visible light is safe
blocks.markdown: Energy per photon depends on frequency. Higher f = higher-energy photons. X-rays can ionize molecules (break bonds). Visible photons can't.

blocks.prompt: What's the wavelength of a microwave oven's 2.4 GHz radiation? (Use c = 3 × 10⁸.) Which EM band is this?
blocks.frame: All EM waves travel at ___ in vacuum. The EM spectrum is ordered from longest wavelength (___) to shortest (___). Visible light is just one narrow band, from ~___ nm to ~___ nm.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **27.1–27.4** (early ideas about light, measuring the speed of light, the electromagnetic spectrum, and why glass is transparent). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
